critique.summary: The article explores whether large language models (LLMs) can accurately predict the temperature change of hot water poured into a ceramic mug over time. The author compares the LLMs' exponential decay equations to actual experimental data and finds that while the models' predictions are reasonable, none perfectly match the observed cooling curve.
mainPoints: The author posed a physics problem about cooling hot water in a ceramic mug to various LLMs, requesting an equation for temperature over time.
LLMs produced equations based on exponential decay terms, modeling fast and slow heat transfer rates.
An experiment was conducted measuring the actual temperature drop of boiling water in a mug at 20°C ambient temperature.
LLM predictions roughly matched the experimental data but were not highly accurate; the water cooled faster early on and slower later than predicted.
The author concludes that while LLMs can approximate physical phenomena, they lack the precision and nuance of real-world experiments.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the complexity of modeling heat transfer in a real-world scenario and correctly notes that LLMs generate approximate mathematical models rather than precise physical predictions. The experimental setup and results are plausible and consistent with known physics of cooling liquids. The description of the LLMs' output as exponential decay functions aligns with typical modeling approaches for cooling processes. There is no indication of fabricated data or false claims. However, the article is a personal experiment and does not cite external scientific sources or peer-reviewed studies, limiting its generalizability.
considerations: The article does not discuss the specific material properties of the mug or environmental conditions like humidity, which affect cooling.
No error analysis or statistical treatment of the experimental data is provided to assess measurement uncertainty.
The LLMs' training data and capabilities regarding physical modeling are not detailed, which could influence their prediction accuracy.
The experiment was conducted once under specific conditions; repeated trials or varying parameters could provide more robust conclusions.
The article lacks references to established heat transfer models or scientific literature for comparison.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion critically evaluates the article's claim about LLMs predicting coffee cooling, highlighting that the experiment mainly models water cooling rather than coffee specifics. Commenters discuss the physical modeling of heat loss, the complexity of the phenomena, and the relevance and novelty of using LLMs for such predictions.

comments.author: drmikeando
comments.text:
To me the neat bit isn't that it got the exponential decay right - that's pretty standard, its that it realised there were two different timescales for the decay and got ball-park numbers for them pretty well.

This is the kind of model you would expect from a simple cylindrical model of the coffee cup with some inbuilt heat capacity of its own.

However, those decay coefficients are going to be very dependent of the physical parameters of your coffee cup - in particular the geometry and thermal parameters of the porcelain. There's a lot of assumptions and variability to account for that the models will have to deal with.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately explains the physical modeling aspect and the significance of identifying multiple decay timescales, showing a good understanding of the underlying physics.

comments.author: shdudns
comments.text:
The problem is both highly complex, but fairly easy to model. Engineers have been doing this for over a century.

Of all the cooling modes identified by the author, one will dominate. And it is almost certainly going to have an exponential relationship with time.

Once this mode decays below the next fastest will this new fastest mode will dominate.

All the LLM has to do, then, is give a reasonable estimate for the Q for:

$T = To exp(-Qt)$

This is not too hard to fit if your training set has the internet within itself.

I would have been more interested to see the equations than the plots, but I would have been most interested to see the plots in log space. There, each cooling mode is a straight line.

The data collected, btw, appears to have at least two exponential modes within it.

[The author did not list the temperature dependance of heat capacity, which for pure water is fairly constant]
comments.analysis: Provides a clear engineering perspective on the cooling process, emphasizing the dominance of one exponential mode and the feasibility of modeling it, aligning well with established knowledge.

comments.author: p4bl0
comments.text: The title is very misleading. This has almost nothing to do with coffee. I was expecting that the input would be the parameters of a coffee recipe (like quantities of coffee and water, grind size, etc for a given type of preparation), and the output to have something to do with coffee too (like extraction time, rate, etc.). It actually is just about water cooling down. Also, it doesn't actually ask the LLM for actual prediction about the result of the experiment, only to generate a ±textbook formula for the situation (which is a good point since LLMs aren't made for that at all, but contributes to make the title misleading).
comments.analysis: Correctly critiques the article's framing and clarifies that the experiment focuses on water cooling physics rather than coffee-specific parameters, improving reader understanding.

comments.author: amha
comments.text:
There's a simple differential equation often taught in intro calc courses, "Newton's Law of Cooling/Heating," which basically says that the rate of heat loss is proportional to the difference in temperature between a substance and its environment. I'm curious what that'd look like here. It's a very simple model, of course, not taking into account all the variables that Dynomight points out, but if a simple model can be nearly as predictive as more complex models...

I'm also curious to see the details of the models that Dynomight's LLMs produced!
comments.analysis: Offers a relevant theoretical reference and expresses interest in model comparisons, contributing constructively to the discussion on model complexity.

comments.author: mycocola
comments.text: The interesting bit about this physical experiment is that the water in the cup never starts at 100 celsius. That the act of pouring significantly reduces temperature is well-documented, so in some sense the LLM output is surprising.
comments.analysis: Highlights an important experimental detail about initial temperature assumptions, adding nuance to the interpretation of the LLM's output.

comments.author: spiralcoaster
comments.text:
Is this for real?

This is like someone with no background in physics or engineering wondering "can a LLM predict the trajectory of my golf ball". They then pontificate about how absolutely complex all of the interacting phenomenon must be! What if there was wind? I didn't tell it what elevation I was at! How could it know the air density!? What if the golf ball wasn't a perfect sphere!!? O M G

And then being amazed when it gets the generic shape of a ballistic curve subject to air resistance.

This speaks far more to the ignorance of the author than something mind boggling about the LLM.
comments.analysis: Critiques the article's surprise at LLM performance, pointing out that generic predictions of well-known physical phenomena are expected, though the tone is somewhat dismissive.

comments.author: detectivestory
comments.text:
On a related note, I have been working on an app that helps determine the correct grinder setting when dialing in espresso. After logging two shots with the same setup (grinder, coffeee machine, basket etc), it then uses machine learning (and some other stuff that I am still improving) to predict the correct setting for your grinder based on the machine temperature, the weight of the shot etc.

https://apps.apple.com/ph/app/grind-finer-app/id6760079211

Its far from perfect when it comes to predictions right now but I expect to have massive improvements over the coming weeks. For now it works ok as an espresso log at least.

I'm hoping after a few tweaks I can save people a lot of wasted coffee!
comments.analysis: Correctly notes that LLMs can reproduce known scientific knowledge from training data, supporting the idea that the output is not surprising.

comments.author: persedes
comments.text: That initial drop reminds me of one of the things that stuck to me from my thermodynamic lectures / tests: If you want to drink coffee at a drinkable temperature in t=15min, will it be colder if you add the milk first or wait 15min and then add milk? (=waiting 15 min because the temperature differential is greater and causes a larger drop). Almost useless fact, but it always comes up when making coffee.
comments.analysis: Shares a relevant thermodynamics anecdote related to coffee temperature, enriching the discussion with practical implications.

comments.author: IncreasePosts
comments.text: The water temperature drops quickly because the room temperature  ceramic mug is getting heated to near equilibrium with the water. If you used a vacuum sealed mug(thermos) then the water temp would drop a bit but not much at all initially.
comments.analysis: Provides a plausible physical explanation for the initial rapid temperature drop, adding useful context to the experiment.

comments.author: foxglacier
comments.text: No estimate of uncertainty in his measurements so he can't really tell who's most right.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid methodological concern about the lack of uncertainty quantification, which is important for evaluating model accuracy.

comments.author: broken-kebab
comments.text: The fact that near boiling water cools down quicker than warm water used to be a well-known kitchen knowledge bit. Like my grandma who wasn't a physicist at all knew it. I guess in some places (particularly those where people microwave water) that part of culture is lost cause there's at least a whole generation which hasn't done cooking.
comments.analysis: Adds cultural and experiential context to the physical phenomenon, supporting the discussion with common knowledge.

comments.author: kaelandt
comments.text: It isn't that surprising that it works well, this problem is fairly well known and some simple heat equations would lead to the result, about which there is a lot of training data online.
comments.analysis: Reinforces that the problem is well understood and that LLMs can leverage abundant data to produce reasonable outputs.

comments.author: e2e4
comments.analysis: A brief, playful comment that adds little substantive analysis to the discussion.

comments.author: leecommamichael
comments.text: ... and so another benchmark is born.
comments.analysis: A short, somewhat sarcastic remark that does not contribute meaningful insight.

comments.author: wallofwonder
comments.text: It looks like the author forgot to insert the joke in the third last paragraph — the author left the placeholder right there in the text! But wait... is the joke forgetting to insert the joke?
comments.analysis: Focuses on a minor editorial oversight unrelated to the technical content, adding no value to the discussion.

comments.author: AuthAuth
comments.text: Irrelevant to your specific cup of coffee its giving you a generic answer.
comments.analysis: A vague comment that does not elaborate on why the answer is generic or its implications.
